data.name: SettingsIntro
data.value: Choose how MoveMentor uses language, the local model and Stockfish on this machine.

data.name: SettingsSetupChecklistBody
data.value: 1. Pick a language. 2. Pick or auto-detect Stockfish. 3. Import a PGN. 4. Run analysis. 5. Follow the training recommendation.

data.name: SettingsUseFullGpu
data.value: Use full GPU power for the local model

data.name: SettingsFooter
data.value: Save when Stockfish is detected or after choosing a custom path.

data.name: SettingsFullGpuDescription
data.value: Full GPU mode is enabled. llama.cpp will request '-ngl {0}', which means pushing all possible model layers onto the GPU.

data.name: SettingsBalancedGpuDescription
data.value: Balanced mode is enabled. llama.cpp will request '-ngl {0}', which is safer on smaller cards and remains the default.

data.name: SettingsStockfishDescription
data.value: Stockfish will use {0} thread(s), {1} MB hash, and bulk PGN analysis will run at depth {2}, MultiPV {3}, {4} ms per position.

data.name: MainDialogNoReplayableGames
data.value: No replayable games were found in the selected PGN file.

data.name: MainDialogLoadedGamesEngineUnavailable
data.value: Loaded {0} games. The analysis engine is unavailable, so bulk analysis cannot start now.

data.name: MainDialogLoadedGames
data.value: Loaded {0} games from the PGN file.

data.name: MainDialogSkippedGames
data.value: Skipped {0} games that could not be parsed or replayed.

data.name: MainDialogNoRecurringPlayer
data.value: No recurring player could be detected, so analysis will use the currently selected side.

data.name: MainDialogDetectedPlayer
data.value: Detected player: {0}. Only that player's moves will be analyzed, as White or Black depending on each game.

data.name: MainDialogAnalyzeNow
data.value: Analyze now? This can take a long time.

data.name: AdviceLanguageInstruction
data.value: Write short_text, detailed_text, and training_hint in {0}. Keep JSON keys and chess notation unchanged.

data.name: AdviceDetailedPartsInstruction
data.value: For detailed_text, use exactly four short parts in this order: {0}:, {1}:, {2}:, {3}:.

data.name: AdviceExampleShortText
data.value: Write a brief summary of the mistake here.

data.name: AdviceExampleDetailedText
data.value: What: Briefly state what went wrong. Why: Briefly explain the core tactical or positional reason. Better: Name the better move and why it helped. Watch next time: Give one pattern to notice earlier.

data.name: AdviceExampleTrainingHint
data.value: Provide a short, actionable rule for the player to remember.

data.name: AdviceTrainingBeginner
data.value: Next time, use one simple habit: {0}

data.name: AdviceTrainingIntermediate
data.value: Watch next time for this trigger: {0}

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedMaterialLoss
data.value: Train this by checking forcing continuations until the resulting material balance is completely clear.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedHangingPiece
data.value: Train board scanning with attacker-defender counts before and after every candidate move.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedMissedTactic
data.value: Train this with short tactical calculation drills that force you to enumerate checks, captures and threats first.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedKingSafety
data.value: Review positions where a single pawn move changed diagonal or file access toward the king.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedOpeningPrinciples
data.value: Review your first 10 moves and justify each one in terms of development, king safety and central influence.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedPieceActivity
data.value: Compare candidate moves by mobility gain, square quality and coordination rather than by surface-level safety alone.

data.name: AdviceTrainingAdvancedEndgameTechnique
data.value: Drill conversion and defensive endgames with emphasis on king activity and zugzwang awareness.

data.name: AdviceHintMaterialLoss
data.value: Before committing, compare the material balance after the forcing reply.

data.name: AdviceHintHangingPiece
data.value: After every move, check whether your moved piece is defended at least as many times as it is attacked.

data.name: AdviceHintKingSafety
data.value: When your king is castled, treat pawn moves in front of it as a concession that needs concrete justification.

data.name: AdviceHintOpeningPrinciples
data.value: In the opening, prefer development, king safety and central control before side pawn moves or early queen adventures.

data.name: AdviceHintPieceActivity
data.value: In quieter middlegames, favor squares that increase your piece activity instead of moves that leave a piece sidelined or passive.

data.name: AdviceHintEndgameTechnique
data.value: In endgames, calculate the simplest technical line and avoid moves that hand activity back to the opponent.

data.name: AdviceHintGeneral
data.value: Pause on tactical turns and look for forcing replies before choosing a natural-looking move.

data.name: AdviceProblemIntermediateCentipawns
data.value: The move {0} was a {1} because it worsened the position by roughly {2} centipawns.

data.name: AdviceProblemIntermediateEvaluation
data.value: The move {0} was a {1} because the position became significantly less healthy afterwards.

data.name: AdviceProblemBeginnerCentipawns
data.value: {0} was a {1} because it gave away roughly {2} centipawns of value.

data.name: AdviceProblemBeginnerEvaluation
data.value: {0} was a {1} because it made your position much harder to handle.

data.name: AdviceProblemAdvancedCentipawns
data.value: {0} qualifies as a {1} because the engine swing is about {2} centipawns and the position loses practical stability.

data.name: AdviceProblemAdvancedEvaluation
data.value: {0} qualifies as a {1} because the evaluation shifts sharply even without a clean centipawn comparison.

data.name: AdviceWhyMaterialLoss
data.value: The main issue was concrete material damage or a line that allowed the opponent to win material cleanly.

data.name: AdviceWhyHangingPiece
data.value: The move left a piece insufficiently defended, so the opponent could challenge it immediately.

data.name: AdviceWhyMissedTactic
data.value: The move missed a forcing tactical resource, either for you or for your opponent in reply.

data.name: AdviceWhyKingSafety
data.value: The move loosened king safety and gave the opponent easier attacking targets or entry lines.

data.name: AdviceWhyOpeningPrinciples
data.value: The move ignored core opening priorities such as development, king safety or central control.

data.name: AdviceWhyPieceActivity
data.value: The move made one of your pieces less active and reduced your ability to fight for key squares.

data.name: AdviceWhyEndgameTechnique
data.value: The move missed a cleaner technical plan and gave away useful activity in the endgame.

data.name: AdviceWhyGeneral
data.value: The move created a practical problem that the opponent could exploit with more active play.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerMaterialLoss
data.value: The biggest problem is that you let the opponent win material.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerHangingPiece
data.value: The moved piece did not have enough protection after the move.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerMissedTactic
data.value: There was a forcing tactical idea in the position that was missed.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerKingSafety
data.value: The move made your king easier to attack.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerOpeningPrinciples
data.value: The move spent time on something less important than development or king safety.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerPieceActivity
data.value: The move placed your piece on a less useful square.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerEndgameTechnique
data.value: The move missed a simpler endgame plan.

data.name: AdviceWhyBeginnerGeneral
data.value: The move allowed the opponent to improve too easily.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedMaterialLoss
data.value: The critical defect is a concrete forcing line in which your position fails the material balance test.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedHangingPiece
data.value: The move leaves the relocated piece tactically underprotected relative to the available attacking resources.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedMissedTactic
data.value: The position contains a forcing tactical resource that should dominate candidate-move selection.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedKingSafety
data.value: The move concedes king shelter, opening tactical access on files, diagonals or weakened color complexes.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedOpeningPrinciples
data.value: The move loses opening efficiency by neglecting development tempo, central influence or timely king safety.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedPieceActivity
data.value: The move lowers activity and coordination, so your piece contributes less to critical squares and plans.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedEndgameTechnique
data.value: The move yields technical control, often by underestimating king activity or the simplest conversion route.

data.name: AdviceWhyAdvancedGeneral
data.value: The move creates an exploitable positional or tactical concession that improves the opponent's options.

data.name: AdviceWatchMaterialLoss
data.value: Before you release the move, scan the forcing captures and ask what material remains after the sequence ends.

data.name: AdviceWatchHangingPiece
data.value: A good trigger is to count attackers and defenders on the destination square before moving on.

data.name: AdviceWatchMissedTactic
data.value: In sharp positions, pause for checks, captures and threats before trusting the first natural move.

data.name: AdviceWatchKingSafety
data.value: Whenever your king shelter changes, double-check opened files, weakened diagonals and loose dark or light squares.

data.name: AdviceWatchOpeningPrinciples
data.value: In the opening, ask whether the move develops a piece, improves king safety or meaningfully fights for the center.

data.name: AdviceWatchPieceActivity
data.value: When no tactic is forcing the game, compare whether your move improves or reduces the mobility of the moved piece.

data.name: AdviceWatchEndgameTechnique
data.value: In endgames, prefer the move that improves king activity or fixes the opponent's weaknesses with the least counterplay.

data.name: AdviceWatchGeneral
data.value: Use the next similar position as a cue to slow down and verify the opponent's strongest forcing reply.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerMaterialLoss
data.value: A simple habit is to ask: after the obvious exchanges, who is up material?

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerHangingPiece
data.value: Before moving on, count how many times the destination square is attacked and defended.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerMissedTactic
data.value: Look for checks, captures and threats before trusting a natural move.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerKingSafety
data.value: Any move near your king should make you ask what attacking lines just opened.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerOpeningPrinciples
data.value: In the opening, first try to develop pieces and get your king safe.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerPieceActivity
data.value: Ask whether the piece will be more active or less active after the move.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerEndgameTechnique
data.value: In the endgame, look for the move that activates your king or improves your simplest plan.

data.name: AdviceWatchBeginnerGeneral
data.value: Use similar moments to slow down and check the opponent's best reply.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedMaterialLoss
data.value: Treat the position as a forcing-tree problem and verify whether the terminal material count still works for you.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedHangingPiece
data.value: Use attacker-defender accounting plus tactical motifs like overload or zwischenzug before accepting the destination square.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedMissedTactic
data.value: Candidate selection should begin with forcing resources, not with the most natural-looking improving move.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedKingSafety
data.value: Re-evaluate access routes to the king immediately after any shelter concession, especially files, diagonals and dark/light square complexes.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedOpeningPrinciples
data.value: Measure opening moves by tempo efficiency, development quality and whether they support a coherent central setup.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedPieceActivity
data.value: Compare candidate moves through activity metrics: mobility, square quality, coordination and restriction of counterplay.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedEndgameTechnique
data.value: In technical endgames, prioritize king activity and the line with the lowest counterplay, even if several moves keep the edge.

data.name: AdviceWatchAdvancedGeneral
data.value: Use the position as a cue to run a stricter candidate-move and forcing-line verification pass.

data.name: AdviceBetterCalmer
data.value: A calmer alternative kept the position healthier.

data.name: AdviceBetterOpeningBeginner
data.value: In {0}, development and king safety mattered more here.

data.name: AdviceBetterOpeningIntermediate
data.value: In {0}, development was the more important priority in this moment.

data.name: AdviceBetterOpeningAdvanced
data.value: In {0}, the better line respected development tempo and opening efficiency.

data.name: HeuristicHintOpening
data.value: Opening: review this pattern in your first 10 moves.

data.name: HeuristicHintMiddlegame
data.value: Middlegame: compare plans and forcing replies before committing.

data.name: HeuristicHintEndgame
data.value: Endgame: activity and technique matter more than cosmetic moves.

data.name: MainReadyEngineLoaded
data.value: MoveMentor Chess is ready. External chess engine loaded from {0}.

data.name: MainReadyEngineUnavailable
data.value: MoveMentor Chess is ready, but the analysis engine is unavailable. {0}

data.name: MainNoLegalMovesForPiece
data.value: No legal moves for this piece.

data.name: MainSelectPieceInspectMoves
data.value: Select a piece to inspect all legal moves.

data.name: MainPrimarySetupStockfish
data.value: Set up Stockfish to unlock analysis

data.name: MainPrimarySetupStockfishDescription
data.value: MoveMentor can still show the board, but engine review and training recommendations need a Stockfish executable.

data.name: MainPrimaryRunAnalysis
data.value: Run analysis and find today's training focus

data.name: MainPrimaryRunAnalysisDescription
data.value: The game is loaded. Analyze it to highlight the moves that cost the most and turn them into practice.

data.name: MainPrimaryLoadPgn
data.value: Load a PGN to start your first review

data.name: MainPrimaryLoadPgnDescription
data.value: Paste a game or load a PGN file. MoveMentor will replay it, analyze mistakes, and suggest what to practice next.

data.name: SavedAnalysesFilterPlaceholder
data.value: Filter by player, opening, result or labels...

data.name: SavedAnalysesNoMatches
data.value: No saved analyses match the current filter.

data.name: SavedAnalysesSelectPrompt
data.value: Select a saved analysis to inspect the cached result.

data.name: SavedAnalysesOpenInstructions
data.value: Use 'Open Analysis' to reopen the full analyzer on cached data, or 'Load Game' to bring the PGN back to the main board.

data.name: SavedAnalysesLoadInstructions
data.value: Use 'Load Game' to bring the PGN back to the main board. Open Analysis is unavailable because the analysis engine is not loaded.

data.name: OpeningTrainerOpeningCatalogHelp
data.value: Choose a specific line when you want to train outside today's lesson.

data.name: OpeningTrainerMoveNotesHelp
data.value: Move notes appear only as short tags while you review the line.

data.name: OpeningTrainerChooseOpeningPreview
data.value: Choose an opening to preview the plan.

data.name: OpeningTrainerPickOpeningCoverage
data.value: Pick an opening to see what is ready and what needs another pass.

data.name: OpeningTrainerHintsPlaceholder
data.value: Hints will appear here when you ask for one.

data.name: PgnImportIntro
data.value: Paste the full PGN file. Once imported, the game will immediately appear on the board and be saved locally.

data.name: SavedGamesLoadHint
data.value: Double click or use Load Selected

data.name: PromotionIntro
data.value: The selected move reaches the back rank. Pick the piece you want to promote to.
